message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    United States government scientists estimate that COVID-19 may kill tens of thousands of Americans. Many of the pre-existing conditions that increase the risk of death in those with COVID-19 are the same diseases that are affected by long-term exposure to air pollution. We investigated whether long-term average exposure to fine particulate matter (PM
                    <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                    ) is associated with an increased risk of COVID-19 death in the United States.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A nationwide, cross-sectional study using county-level data.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 death counts were collected for more than 3,000 counties in the United States (representing 98% of the population) up to April 22, 2020 from Johns Hopkins University, Center for Systems Science and Engineering Coronavirus Resource Center.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We fit negative binomial mixed models using county-level COVID-19 deaths as the outcome and county-level long-term average of PM
                    <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                    as the exposure. In the main analysis, we adjusted by 20 potential confounding factors including population size, age distribution, population density, time since the beginning of the outbreak, time since state’s issuance of stay-at-home order, hospital beds, number of individuals tested, weather, and socioeconomic and behavioral variables such as obesity and smoking. We included a random intercept by state to account for potential correlation in counties within the same state. We conducted more than 68 additional sensitivity analyses.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We found that an increase of only 1
                    <jats:italic>μ</jats:italic>
                    g/m
                    <jats:sup>3</jats:sup>
                    in PM
                    <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                    is associated with an 8% increase in the COVID-19 death rate (95% confidence interval [CI]: 2%, 15%). The results were statistically significant and robust to secondary and sensitivity analyses.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A small increase in long-term exposure to PM
                    <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                    leads to a large increase in the COVID-19 death rate. Despite the inherent limitations of the ecological study design, our results underscore the importance of continuing to enforce existing air pollution regulations to protect human health both during and after the COVID-19 crisis. The data and code are publicly available so our analyses can be updated routinely.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Summary Box</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known on this topic</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="order">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          Long-term exposure to PM
                          <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                          is linked to many of the comorbidities that have been associated with poor prognosis and death in COVID-19 patients, including cardiovascular and lung disease.
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          PM
                          <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                          exposure is associated with increased risk of severe outcomes in patients with certain infectious respiratory diseases, including influenza, pneumonia, and SARS.
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Air pollution exposure is known to cause inflammation and cellular damage, and evidence suggests that it may suppress early immune response to infection.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What this study adds</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="order">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          This is the first nationwide study of the relationship between historical exposure to air pollution exposure and COVID-19 death rate, relying on data from more than 3,000 counties in the United States. The results suggest that long-term exposure to PM
                          <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                          is associated with higher COVID-19 mortality rates, after adjustment for a wide range of socioeconomic, demographic, weather, behavioral, epidemic stage, and healthcare-related confounders.
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>This study relies entirely on publicly available data and fully reproducible, public code to facilitate continued investigation of these relationships by the broader scientific community as the COVID-19 outbreak evolves and more data become available.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                    <jats:p>
                      A small increase in long-term PM
                      <jats:sup>2.5</jats:sup>
                      exposure was associated with a substantial increase in the county’s COVID-19 mortality rate up to April 22, 2020.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Exposure to air pollution and COVID-19 mortality in the United States: A nationwide cross-sectional study

reference.unstructured: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. National Vital Statistics System. Provisional Death Counts for Coronavirus Disease (COVID-19) Available from: https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nvss/vsrr/COVID19/.

Diamond M , Peniston Feliciano HL , Sanghavi D , et al. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) [Updated 2020 Jan 5]. StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island, FL: StatPearls Publishing 2020. Available from https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/.
Health Effects Institute. State of Global Air 2019 2019 Available from: www.stateofglobalair.org accessed April 23 2020.
United States Environmental Protection Agency. Integrated Science Assessment (ISA) for Particulate Matter (Final Report, 2019). EPA/600/R-19/188. Washington, DC: US EPA 2019.

reference.article-title: Fine particulate air pollution and human mortality: 25+ years of cohort studies

reference.unstructured: R Core Team. R: A language and environment for statistical computing. Vienna, Austria: R Foundation for Statistical Computing 2020. Available from: https://www.R-project.org/.
Bolker B. glmer.nb Fitting Negative Binomial GLMMs Available from: https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/lme4/versions/1.1-23/topics/glmer.nb.
